title: Who Is Winning Africa’s Drone Wars?
gist: Allen and Lyammouri document the collapse of state monopoly over drone warfare in Africa: at least 37 militaries and nonstate actors in 17 countries now use cheap drones, with insurgents often out-innovating governments; the authors argue dependence on foreign drone suppliers (now led by China) is destabilizing and that African states must build domestic drone industries integrated with ground forces.

themes.theme: Collapse of the state drone monopoly

insights.claim: In June 2025 a Tuareg rebel group used cheap fiber-optic FPV drones, a technology that first appeared in Ukraine barely a year earlier, to destroy a Russian Africa Corps and Malian convoy, showing a resource-poor nonstate actor matching the cutting edge.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: The diffusion lag from frontier battlefield to global insurgents is now ~1 year; cheap drone tech equalizes resource asymmetries fast.
insights.quote: a little over a year after fiber-optic drones first appeared in Ukraine, a rebel group with scant resources had deployed the cutting-edge technology

insights.claim: The US monopoly on African drone strikes (a $20 million Predator in 2011) has collapsed: at least 37 African militaries have acquired drones, and nonstate actors have used armed drones in at least 17 countries.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: Air dominance is no longer a state preserve; cost collapse from $20M systems to assemblable parts reshapes conflict economics globally.
insights.quote: at least 37 African militaries have acquired drones of varying sophistication and cost

insights.claim: In Libya (2020 Tripoli battle) and Ethiopia (2020-22 Tigray war), a small number of capable drones like the Bayraktar TB2 decided conflicts by repelling exposed, concentrated invading forces, the model that seduced other African states.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: Drone decisiveness depends on enemy concentration; states wrongly generalized this to dispersed insurgencies.
insights.quote: It was those fucking drones . . . . We had no way of shooting them down.
insights.locator: AIR TRAFFIC CONTROL / 'Drones played an even more consequential role'

insights.claim: In the Sahel, Mali, Burkina Faso, and Niger juntas' heavy use of Turkish drones has coincided with eroding territorial control, as JNIM and Islamic State Sahel Province advance and spread into previously stable northern Benin, Cote d'Ivoire, Nigeria, and Togo.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: Airpower substituted for ground forces and governance produces strategic decline; a warning against capital-intensive tech as a counterinsurgency shortcut.
insights.quote: the juntas' extensive use of drones in the fight against these insurgents has coincided with the erosion of their control over their own territory
insights.locator: WE'RE NOT IN ADDIS ANYMORE / 'Yet unlike in Libya or Ethiopia'

insights.claim: Insurgents nullify state air superiority by dispersing into small units, swapping convoys for motorcycles and farm carts to blend with civilians, and weaponizing civilian-casualty drone strikes for recruitment, as JNIM and Boko Haram have done.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: Cheap evasion tactics blunt expensive airpower and turn collateral damage into a recruiting asset, inverting the cost-exchange ratio.
insights.quote: al-Shabab fighters have eschewed convoys of land cruisers for motorcycles and wooden farm carts
insights.locator: WE'RE NOT IN ADDIS ANYMORE / 'In Somalia, for example'

insights.claim: Close to 90 percent of Africa's military drones come from external suppliers, and as US sales declined since 2020 China has become the continent's largest supplier, accounting for a quarter of drones procured by African states.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: Drone sales are reshaping Africa's defense partnerships toward China and middle powers; supplier leverage translates into trade and military influence.
insights.quote: Close to 90 percent of Africa's military drones... come from external suppliers
insights.locator: WEAPONIZED DEPENDENCE / 'While insurgent groups gain proficiency'

insights.claim: Cheap exportable drones let outside powers fuel proxy wars without direct intervention, as in Sudan where Turkish and Iranian drones aided the SAF and UAE-supplied suicide drones backed the RSF, contributing to at least 150,000 dead and 12 million displaced.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: Drone exports prolong and intensify conflicts at low cost to sponsors; a structural driver of African instability and displacement.
insights.quote: drones have made it easier for outside powers to prolong conflicts without paying the price typically associated with direct military intervention
insights.locator: WEAPONIZED DEPENDENCE / 'Because foreign sponsors can sell drones'

insights.claim: At least 11 African countries already produce drones, including Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, and Morocco (which announced coproduction with Israel and the US); the authors argue others should reallocate foreign-procurement budgets to low-cost attritable domestic drones.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: Domestic drone industry is the prescribed path to defense resilience and leverage; watch for critical-mineral-for-knowhow deals.
insights.quote: At least 11 African countries already produce drones
insights.locator: BUY LOCAL / 'At least 11 African countries already produce drones'

insights.claim: A thriving African drone manufacturing sector, drawing on local labor and raw materials, could offer meaningful competition to China's heavily state-subsidized dominance over global commercial drones and components.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: A potential long-term challenge to Chinese drone supply-chain dominance, relevant to global drone-component sourcing.
insights.quote: China has established dominance over the global supply of commercial drones and components through heavy state subsidies.
insights.locator: BUY LOCAL / 'Greater control over drone production'

insights.claim: As the drone revolution continues, integration into existing force structures and ground capabilities, not technological supremacy, will be the key determinant of conflict outcomes.
insights.speaker: Nate Allen and Rida Lyammouri
insights.so_what: The strategic edge shifts from owning advanced hardware to organizational integration, the same lesson Kofman draws for NATO.
insights.quote: the equalizing effect of low-cost drones will make integration into existing capabilities, systems, and force structures—not technological supremacy—the key determinant
insights.locator: Closing / 'And as the drone revolution continues apace'
